section.title: Gethsemane, Arrest, and Peter's Denial
section.sectionRationale: On the Mount of Olives Jesus prays in agony, asking that the cup pass yet surrendering to the Father's will, strengthened by an angel while His disciples sleep. Judas arrives with a crowd and betrays Him with a kiss; Jesus heals the wounded servant and gives Himself to the hour of darkness. As He is led away, Peter denies three times that he knows Him, and the Lord's look breaks his heart. Mocked and beaten through the night, Jesus stands before the Sanhedrin and confesses that from now on the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of God.
section.thesis: In the garden and before His judges Jesus embraces the cup of suffering in perfect surrender to the Father, refusing the sword and healing His enemy, so that even in betrayal, denial, and mockery He goes as the willing, reigning Son of Man to His appointed hour.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the agony and prayer in Gethsemane; the betrayal and arrest; Peter's threefold denial and the Lord's look; and the mocking and the confession before the Sanhedrin.

units.label: The agony in Gethsemane
units.note: not My will, but Yours be done

verses.text: Jesus went out as usual to the Mount of Olives, and the disciples followed Him.
When He came to the place, He told them, “Pray that you will not enter into {{peirasmos|temptation}}.”
And He withdrew about a stone’s throw beyond them, where He knelt down and prayed,
“Father, if You are willing, take this {{poterion|cup}} from Me. Yet not My {{thelema|will}}, but Yours be done.”
Then an angel from heaven appeared to Him and strengthened Him.
And in His anguish, He prayed more earnestly, and His sweat became like drops of blood falling to the ground.
When Jesus rose from prayer and returned to the disciples, He found them asleep, exhausted from sorrow.
“Why are you sleeping?” He asked. “Get up and pray so that you will not enter into temptation.”

units.label: The betrayal and arrest
units.note: this hour belongs to the power of darkness

verses.text: While He was still speaking, a crowd arrived, led by the man called Judas, one of the Twelve. He approached Jesus to kiss Him.
But Jesus asked him, “Judas, are you betraying the Son of Man with a kiss?”
Those around Jesus saw what was about to happen and said, “Lord, should we strike with our swords?”
And one of them struck the servant of the high priest, cutting off his right ear.
But Jesus answered, “No more of this!” And He touched the man’s ear and healed him.
Then Jesus said to the chief priests, temple officers, and elders who had come for Him, “Have you come out with swords and clubs as you would against an outlaw?
Every day I was with you in the temple courts, and you did not lay a hand on Me. But this hour belongs to you and to the power of darkness.”

units.label: Peter's denial
units.note: the Lord turned and looked at Peter

verses.text: Then they seized Jesus, led Him away, and took Him into the house of the high priest. And Peter followed at a distance.
When those present had kindled a fire in the middle of the courtyard and sat down together, Peter sat down among them.
A servant girl saw him seated in the firelight and looked intently at him. “This man also was with Him,” she said.
But Peter denied it. “Woman, I do not know Him,” he said.
A short time later, someone else saw him and said, “You also are one of them.” But Peter said, “Man, I am not.”
About an hour later, another man insisted, “Certainly this man was with Him, for he too is a Galilean.”
“Man, I do not know what you are talking about,” Peter replied. And immediately, while he was still speaking, the rooster crowed.
And the Lord turned and looked at Peter. Then Peter remembered the word that the Lord had spoken to him: “Before the rooster crows today, you will deny Me three times.”
And he went outside and wept bitterly.

units.label: The mocking and the Sanhedrin
units.note: from now on the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of God

verses.text: The men who were holding Jesus began to mock Him and beat Him.
They blindfolded Him and kept demanding, “Prophesy! Who hit You?”
And they said many other blasphemous things against Him.
At daybreak the council of the elders of the people, both the chief priests and scribes, met together. They led Jesus into their Sanhedrin and said,
“If You are the {{christos|Christ}}, tell us.” Jesus answered, “If I tell you, you will not believe.
And if I ask you a question, you will not answer.
But from now on the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of the power of God.”
So they all asked, “Are You then the Son of God?” He replied, “You say that I am.”
“Why do we need any more testimony?” they declared. “We have heard it for ourselves from His own lips.”

peirasmos.gloss: Temptation, testing, trial. Twice Jesus tells the disciples to pray not to enter into temptation, for the hour of testing is upon them all. He Himself faces the ultimate trial in the garden and overcomes it by prayer and surrender, while they sleep and soon fall, one to denial. Prayer is the appointed guard against the coming test.

poterion.gloss: Cup. In the prophets the cup is the cup of God's wrath against sin. Jesus asks the Father, if You are willing, take this cup from Me, then drinks it in obedience. The cup He shrinks from and yet accepts is the suffering of bearing sin and judgment in His people's place.

thelema.gloss: Will, desire, purpose. Not My will, but Yours be done is the heart of Gethsemane and the reversal of Eden's grasping. The Son surrenders His own recoiling will to the Father's saving purpose, and in that obedience our salvation is secured. It is the prayer He taught His disciples, lived out in agony.

christos.gloss: Christ, Messiah, the Anointed One. The council demands to know if Jesus is the Christ, and He answers by declaring that the Son of Man will be seated at God's right hand, claiming a divine, reigning messiahship far beyond their categories. His confession, made under judgment, is the truth for which they condemn Him and the truth by which we are saved.

basic.context: Jesus goes to the Mount of Olives and tells the disciples to pray not to fall into temptation. Alone, He kneels and prays that if the Father is willing, the cup would pass, yet surrenders, not My will but Yours be done. An angel strengthens Him, and in anguish His sweat falls like drops of blood, while the disciples sleep from sorrow. Judas arrives with a crowd and betrays Him with a kiss; when a disciple cuts off the servant's ear, Jesus says no more of this and heals him, then gives Himself to the hour of darkness. Peter follows at a distance, warms himself at the fire, and three times denies knowing Jesus. The rooster crows, the Lord turns and looks at him, and Peter goes out and weeps bitterly. Through the night Jesus is mocked and beaten, and at daybreak He tells the Sanhedrin that the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of God, confessing Himself the Christ and Son of God.
basic.hermeneutics: Gethsemane is the interpretive center: Jesus' surrender of His will secures the salvation His death accomplishes. The arrest, denial, and trial then unfold against this obedience, contrasting His steadfast faithfulness with the disciples' sleep, Peter's denial, and the leaders' blasphemy, while His confession reveals who is truly on trial.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus warns of peirasmos, the coming temptation, and meets His own by prayer. He asks the Father to remove the poterion, the cup of wrath, yet yields with not My thelema, My will, but Yours. And before the council He is asked whether He is the christos, and answers by claiming the Son of Man's seat at God's right hand.
basic.typology: The cup of wrath from the prophets is drunk by Christ in the sinner's place. His surrender in the garden reverses Adam's grasping in Eden. The Son of Man seated at God's right hand fulfills Daniel and Psalm 110, revealing the condemned prisoner as the coming Judge.
basic.christ: Jesus is the obedient Son who drinks the cup He dreads, the King who will not be defended by the sword and heals His captor's servant, and the Son of Man who confesses His coming enthronement even as He is condemned. His faithfulness where Adam and the disciples failed is the ground of salvation.
basic.god: God's will is the saving purpose to which the Son yields, and God sends an angel to strengthen Him. God ordains the hour of darkness without being its author, and God will vindicate His Son by seating Him at His right hand. The Father's will and the Son's obedience together accomplish redemption.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 51 speaks of the cup of God's wrath. Psalm 110 seats the Lord at God's right hand. Daniel 7 gives the Son of Man's dominion. Hebrews 5 says Jesus learned obedience through what He suffered, with loud cries and tears.
basic.oneStory: In the garden the second Adam surrenders His will to the Father where the first Adam grasped, and He drinks the cup of wrath for His people. Betrayed, denied, and mocked, He goes willingly to the cross as the reigning Son of Man, so the story turns on His obedience, the hinge on which our salvation swings.

context.literary: This section forms the heart of Luke's passion narrative, moving from prayer to arrest to denial to trial in a single night. Luke uniquely records the strengthening angel and the sweat like blood, the healing of the servant's ear, and the Lord's turning to look at Peter. The confession before the Sanhedrin about the Son of Man at God's right hand is the theological climax of the trial.
context.historical: The Mount of Olives, with its garden of Gethsemane, was Jesus' customary retreat. A betrayal kiss, an armed crowd at night, and a hurried nocturnal hearing reflect the leaders' fear of the crowd. The Sanhedrin was the ruling council; a claim to sit at God's right hand would be heard as blasphemy. Peter's Galilean accent gave him away in the high priest's courtyard.
context.canonical: The cup of wrath is a prophetic image Jesus takes up and drains. The Son of Man at God's right hand fuses Daniel 7 and Psalm 110, the two texts most used in the New Testament for Christ's exaltation. Gethsemane's obedient prayer is echoed in Hebrews and stands as the counterpoint to Adam's disobedience developed by Paul in Romans.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To display Jesus' perfect obedience in surrendering His will to the Father and drinking the cup of suffering, and to reveal that in betrayal, denial, and unjust trial He remains the willing, reigning Son of Man who goes to the cross by the Father's saving purpose.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Jesus' unique saving obedience, which cannot be repeated, yet His command to pray against temptation and His pattern of surrendered prayer are held up for imitation. Peter's denial is a warning, and Jesus' refusal of the sword shapes how His people meet hostility.

debates.question: What is the cup Jesus asks the Father to remove?

views.label: The cup of divine wrath against sin
views.case: In the prophets the cup is God's judgment poured out on sin. Jesus recoils not merely from physical death but from bearing the wrath due His people, drinking the cup of judgment in their place. His agony reflects the horror of becoming sin-bearer, and His acceptance secures atonement.

views.label: The whole ordeal of suffering and death
views.case: Some understand the cup more broadly as the entire experience of betrayal, torture, and crucifixion that lay before Him. On this reading His anguish is the fully human dread of a horrific death, met and mastered by trust in the Father.

debates.bearing: Either way the garden reveals the real cost of the cross and the depth of Jesus' obedience; His surrender, not My will but Yours, is what carries Him to drink it for us.

debates.question: Why does Jesus answer the Sanhedrin as He does about the Son of Man?

views.label: A deliberate claim to divine, reigning messiahship
views.case: By fusing Daniel 7 and Psalm 110, Jesus claims to be the Son of Man who will sit at God's right hand and come in judgment. It is a confession of a messiahship far higher than a mere national deliverer, which the council rightly hears as a claim to share God's throne, and so condemns as blasphemy.

views.label: A turning of the tables on His judges
views.case: Jesus reframes the trial: though He stands condemned before them now, from now on they will face Him as the enthroned Son of Man. The One being judged is in truth the coming Judge, so His answer is both confession and warning to the court.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that Jesus confesses His true identity under oath: the condemned prisoner is the Christ, the Son of God, and the Son of Man soon to be seated in power at God's right hand.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus twice warns the disciples to pray lest they enter peirasmos, the hour of testing that is about to fall on them all, and He meets His own supreme trial not by avoidance but by prayer. He asks the Father to take away the poterion, the cup, which in the prophets is the cup of God's wrath against sin, and His anguish shows how real is the horror of drinking it in the place of sinners. Yet the decisive word is thelema: not My will, but Yours be done, the Son bending His recoiling human will to the Father's saving purpose, reversing the grasping self-will of Eden. And before the council, asked whether He is the christos, the Messiah, He answers by declaring that the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of the power of God, claiming a divine and reigning messiahship, the very confession for which they condemn Him and by which His people are saved.

typology.type: The cup of God's wrath in the prophets, Isaiah 51:17
typology.fulfillment: The cup Jesus dreads and drinks in the sinner's place, Luke 22:42.

typology.type: Adam grasping his own will in Eden
typology.fulfillment: The second Adam surrendering, not My will but Yours be done, Luke 22:42.

typology.type: The Son of Man given dominion at God's right hand, Daniel 7 and Psalm 110
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' confession that the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of God, Luke 22:69.

study.christ: Jesus is the obedient Son whose agony in the garden is real, sweat like blood and a soul that recoils from the cup, yet who surrenders wholly to the Father's will and so secures the salvation His death will accomplish. He is the King who forbids the sword and heals the ear of the very servant sent to seize Him, meeting evil with mercy to the last. He is the faithful witness who, mocked and blindfolded and beaten, does not waver, and who, standing condemned before the Sanhedrin, confesses that the Son of Man will be enthroned at God's right hand. Where Adam grasped and the disciples slept and Peter denied, Jesus obeyed, and His faithfulness is the ground on which sinners stand.
study.god: The God of Gethsemane is the Father whose will is a saving purpose so weighty that the Son sweats blood to embrace it, and who tenderly sends an angel to strengthen Him in the struggle. He ordains the hour of darkness for the redemption of the world without Himself being the author of the evil that fills it, holding betrayer and council responsible even as His plan advances. And He is the God who will vindicate His obedient Son, seating Him at His own right hand in power. The Father who does not spare the Son, and the Son who yields to the Father, together accomplish the salvation of a lost world.

crossReferences.note: The cup of God's wrath that Jerusalem drank, the cup Jesus takes up in His people's place.
The LORD said to my Lord, sit at My right hand, the throne Jesus claims before the Sanhedrin.
The Son of Man given everlasting dominion, fused with Psalm 110 in Jesus' confession.
Jesus offered up prayers with loud cries and tears and learned obedience through suffering, the garden's agony.

study.oneStory: In the garden the second Adam kneels where the first Adam grasped, and surrenders His will to the Father, taking up the cup of wrath for His people. Betrayed with a kiss, denied by His friend, mocked and unjustly tried, He never ceases to be the willing and reigning Son of Man, healing His enemy and confessing His coming enthronement even under condemnation. The whole story of salvation turns on this obedience, as the faithful Son goes willingly toward the cross where the cup will be drained to the dregs.

quiz.q: How does Jesus pray in Gethsemane?

options.text: He asks the Father to take the cup if He is willing, yet surrenders, not My will but Yours be done.
options.note: Luke 22:42 holds together the plea and the full surrender to the Father's will.

options.text: He asks that the cup of suffering be taken from Him.
options.note: True, but it omits the decisive surrender, not My will but Yours be done.

options.text: He calmly accepts His fate without any struggle.
options.note: His prayer is one of real anguish, sweat like blood, not detached calm.

options.text: He asks the Father to destroy His enemies.
options.note: He prays about the cup and surrenders to the Father's will, not for vengeance.

quiz.q: How does Jesus respond to the sword at His arrest?

options.text: He says 'No more of this' and heals the servant whose ear was cut off.
options.note: Luke 22:51 shows Jesus halting the violence and healing His captor's servant.

options.text: He tells the disciples to stop fighting.
options.note: True, but it omits that He healed the wounded servant, mercy to His enemy.

options.text: He allows the disciples to defend Him with swords.
options.note: The opposite; He forbids further violence and undoes the harm done.

options.text: He calls down angels to strike the crowd.
options.note: He surrenders to the hour of darkness rather than resisting by force.

quiz.q: What happens after Peter denies Jesus three times?

options.text: The rooster crows, the Lord turns and looks at Peter, and Peter goes out and weeps bitterly.
options.note: Luke 22:60-62 records the crowing, the Lord's look, and Peter's bitter weeping.

options.text: Peter remembers Jesus' words and is filled with grief.
options.note: True, but it omits the piercing detail of the Lord turning to look at him.

options.text: Peter immediately confesses Jesus before the crowd.
options.note: He weeps in private repentance; he does not at once confess Christ openly.

options.text: Peter feels no remorse for his denial.
options.note: He weeps bitterly, deeply grieved by his failure.

quiz.q: What does Jesus confess before the Sanhedrin?

options.text: That from now on the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of the power of God.
options.note: Luke 22:69 is the climactic claim to divine, reigning messiahship.

options.text: That He is the Christ and the Son of God.
options.note: True from their questions and His reply, but it omits the specific claim to the throne at God's right hand.

options.text: That He is merely a teacher sent from God.
options.note: He claims far more, a seat at the right hand of God's power.

options.text: That He is not the Christ at all.
options.note: He affirms His identity, which is why they condemn Him.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus tell the disciples to pray in the garden?

options.text: So that they will not enter into temptation, the hour of testing about to fall on them.
options.note: Luke 22:40,46 gives the reason: prayer against the coming temptation.

options.text: So that they would stay awake and alert with Him.
options.note: True in effect, but the stated purpose is to be kept from falling into temptation.

options.text: So that they could ask God to spare Jesus from arrest.
options.note: The prayer He commands is for themselves against temptation, not to prevent His arrest.

options.text: Because prayer was simply their evening habit.
options.note: He gives a specific, urgent reason: the coming hour of testing.

questions: Jesus surrendered His will with, not My will but Yours be done. Where is God asking you to yield a desire you are gripping tightly, and what makes that surrender hard?
In His deepest agony Jesus prayed, while the disciples slept. When sorrow or exhaustion weighs on you, do you turn to prayer or drift into a spiritual sleep?
Jesus healed the ear of the man sent to arrest Him. How does His mercy toward an enemy challenge the way you respond to those who wrong you?
The Lord turned and looked at Peter, and it broke him. Have you known that kind of look from Christ, grieved and yet loving, that leads to repentance rather than despair?
Standing condemned, Jesus confessed the truth of who He is. Where are you tempted to stay silent about Christ when honesty would be costly?

translationNotes.note: Some early manuscripts do not include the verses about the strengthening angel and the sweat like drops of blood. The BSB retains them; they vividly convey the reality of Jesus' agony and the Father's provision of strength.
'This cup' draws on the prophetic image of the cup of God's wrath against sin. Jesus prays for its removal yet surrenders to drink it, bearing the judgment due His people.

Jesus fuses Daniel 7:13 and Psalm 110:1, claiming that the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of God's power. The council hears this as a claim to share God's throne and condemns it as blasphemy.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 22:39-71. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text; no em or en dashes occur in this passage. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
